back: A system that stores data as tables of rows and columns, queried with SQL. The backbone of business computing since the 1970s.
Structured Query Language — the standard language for asking questions of and changing data in relational systems.
Online Transaction Processing: many small, fast, exactly-correct reads and writes, like recording orders or payments.
Online Analytical Processing: large, read-heavy queries that summarize lots of data for reporting and business intelligence.
A separate system devoted to analysis (OLAP), with data copied in and organized for reporting and dashboards.
Requiring data's structure (columns and types) to be defined before loading it. Tidy, but rigid.
Handling more load by buying a bigger, more powerful machine — expensive, and it eventually hits a ceiling.
Data stored in one vendor's closed format, making it hard to use with other tools and costly to move.
Structured data fits neat rows and columns; unstructured data (text, images, video, logs) does not.
Rigid structure up front, expensive scale-up, closed formats, and no good home for unstructured data or machine learning.
